name: African Blue Lily

symptoms.name: Immediate intense pain and local irritation to mucous membranes
symptoms.notes: This is caused by insoluble calcium oxalate crystals found in all parts of the plant (especially the rhizomes) and a sticky, acrid latex. The pain usually limits the amount a cat will swallow3. 2. Excessive Drooling (Hypersalivation)

symptoms.name: Excess salivation
symptoms.notes: This occurs as a physiological response to the mechanical irritation and burning sensation in the mouth3. 3. Swelling of the Mouth and Throat

symptoms.name: Swollen tongue and pharynx (throat)
symptoms.notes: The inflammation is a direct result of the calcium oxalate crystals embedding in the soft tissues3. 4. Difficulty Breathing (Dyspnea)

symptoms.name: Dyspnea
symptoms.notes: Breathing difficulties arise if the swelling of the tongue and pharynx becomes significant enough to obstruct the airway3. 5. Gastrointestinal Upset

symptoms.name: Diarrhea
symptoms.notes: The plant contains an irritant latex that disrupts the GI tract. Unlike true lilies, it does not cause acute renal failure

chemicals.name: Insoluble Calcium Oxalate Crystals
chemicals.description: in cats: These crystals act as mechanical irritants. Upon ingestion, they cause immediate and intense pain and local irritation to the mucous membranes of the mouth and throat. This physical trauma leads to clinical signs such as excessive salivation (drooling), swelling of the tongue and pharynx, and difficulty breathing (dyspnea)1. 4. Notes on
chemicals.concentration_notes: These crystals are found in all parts of the plant, but they are notably concentrated in the rhizomes (the underground root-like stems)

chemicals.name: Sticky, acrid, irritant latex (The specific toxic principle within this latex is currently unknown)
chemicals.description: in cats: This substance acts as a gastrointestinal and mucosal irritant. In addition to oral pain, ingestion can lead to gastrointestinal upset, specifically diarrhea1. 4. Notes on
chemicals.concentration_notes: While specific potency data is not listed, the latex is described as "acrid" and contributes to the immediate painful reaction that usually limits the amount a pet will ingest1. Important Note on Toxicity Type: It is critical to distinguish the African Blue Lily from "true lilies" (Lilium species). treatments.name: Airway Management and Oxygen Support
treatments.description: The veterinarian secures the animal's airway to ensure they can breathe, potentially administering oxygen if necessary
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent intervention if the cat is experiencing dyspnea (difficulty breathing) due to severe swelling of the tongue and pharynx (throat), which are documented symptoms of Agapanthus ingestion

treatments.name: Oral Decontamination
treatments.description: Physical removal of any remaining plant material from the mouth, followed by rinsing or wiping the oral cavity with water
treatments.notes: This step aims to flush out the insoluble calcium oxalate crystals and the sticky, acrid irritant latex found in the plant (especially the rhizomes) that cause immediate intense pain and local irritation

treatments.name: Administration of Calcium-Containing Products
treatments.description: Oral administration of milk or other calcium-containing dairy products
treatments.notes: Insoluble oxalates often bind to the calcium in dairy products; feeding this to the cat can aid in clearing the crystals from the oral cavity and esophagus, helping to soothe the irritation

treatments.name: Symptomatic Supportive Care
treatments.description: Administration of medications to manage specific symptoms, such as diarrhea or pain
treatments.notes: Since the plant acts as a gastrointestinal irritant causing excess salivation and diarrhea, care is "symptomatic," meaning it targets these specific signs as they resolve16. safetyNotes: Symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, and excessive drooling are the most common signs of ingestion.
Not a true lily: Unlike Lilium or Hemerocallis species, this plant does not cause kidney failure, making it significantly less dangerous — but it is still not safe to ingest.
Action: Monitor your cat after any suspected ingestion; contact a vet if symptoms persist or worsen.
description: African Blue Lily (Agapanthus) is often mistaken for a true lily, but it belongs to a different plant family and does not cause the acute kidney failure associated with true lilies (Lilium and Hemerocallis species). However, it is mildly toxic to cats and can cause gastrointestinal distress when any part of the plant — including the leaves, stems, or roots — is ingested. Cats are sometimes attracted to its grass-like foliage, so households with this plant should monitor access.
